posts.name: Andrew Will
posts.cooked:
Hey folks: I received a shipment of wine last night, including two bottles of 1996 Bel-Air Marquis d’Aligre. Oddly enough, both labels were lopsided and one even fell off after unpackaging. I also noticed that the capsules differ from those I’ve seen on other 1996s.

Does anyone have any insight as to why this might be? I don’t want to assume they are fakes. I understand that Mr. Boyer is peculiar in his storage and bottling habits, so it may simply be a case of unique bottling. But this is my first experience with this importer and I want to ensure it’s all above-board (the 2000 Bramaterras I bought are incredible, which is a good sign).

I coravin’ed a small glass from one of the bottles. It smelled and tasted like mature claret, but nothing exceptional. Clearly I did not decant for the recommended four to six hours, however.

Any advice?

IMG_9572.HEIC3024×4032 2.87 MB

IMG_9573.HEIC3024×4032 2.01 MB

IMG_9574.HEIC3024×4032 2.33 MB

user_status.description: Yes I have the same name as the wine. No we are not related.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Estate winding down and selling library stock, hence generic Bordeaux capsules.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
Not sure if it would have any bearing on the 1996 bottling, but my 2010s all arrived from Wine Exchange with the labels falling off/peeling back on the same side.

*Edit - That does look like a crazy low fill level on one of the bottles in the first photo. Not sure if inconsistent fill level is to be expected, or if that could be a cork issue… The cork on the 1996 I brought home from Bordeaux didn’t fare too well, but I didn’t get any (noticeable) seepage.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Old news… 2012 is the last vintage produced AFAIK. Vines going to Claire Villars-Lurton @ Ferrière.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I have never understood the fetishization of long decants/opening times for BAMA, but if Boyer is/was actually advising this, it makes more sense.

For me, the wines are pretty polished, which is no surprise given the very long élevage, absence of oak tannin and lack of exaggerated extraction, and I have typically found that 1h30 in a decanter is just fine.

1986 from my cellar is pretty great with very brief air time, though I prefer 82 by some margin.

Have a magnum of 1961 lined up for some time soon, just need to make more friends

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: You have so many virtual friends here.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Old news… 2012 is the last vintage produced AFAIK. Vines going to Claire Villars-Lurton @ Ferrière.

Glad to hear this, love Ferriere. Just grabbed some 2000 and more 2019 last week.

What vintage of Ferriere will start including Boyer’s vines?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I’m not sure about the fetishization, but I have found on many vintages of his wines that they actually taste better the next day. Granted, that is me opening a bottle, drinking half of it, putting half in the fridge, and enjoying the remainder of it the next evening. I’m not sure I would ever do a six, 12 or overnight decant, if that is what some people are recommending, but I would not ordinarily do that to any wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think that has been the case for some time, given that there is no BAMA post 2012 as far as I know.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

I think that has been the case for some time, given that there is no BAMA post 2012 as far as I know.

Perhaps that partly explains a step up in quality, the 2016 is outstanding and the 2019 is just a small notch below.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
This was our lallocation this year, 12 is the last vintage. we did not take 1969, 1970 was sold out. It was a shame, there was no 1985 as that was really good. It is funny doing business with him, one places the order, he sends the invoice, one sends the cheque. Two or three months later the order arrives, never any warning, and then four or five months later he cashes the check.
We took the 1969 to a tasting with Leclpart and some other natural producers, they could not beleive this was Bordeaux.

Boyer is very difficult to understand, a very thick bordelaise accent, my french colleague does not even really understand him. He always gives advice what to drink and when. He told us his preference is to open five days in advance, this we have followed and the evolution is amazing.
Boyer advised us to drink the 2011 now, the 11 one can pop and pour, it is a light wieght BAMA, has all the aromatics. 12 in contrast is a monster.

CHT BEL AIR MARQUIS ALIGRE 05 MARGAU

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: According to Neal Martin’s report a few years back, he did make some 2015, which Neal tasted from vat, but it has never been released. Probably never will. It is sad indeed but not exactly a surprise and now’s the time to buy what you can, I would say.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The 1970 is very good, but one of the most delicate I have had. I had a few bottles and the first I regretted decanting. Of course the last was corked.

There is clearly a difference between lots / late release from the estate vs early release.

09 and 10 are the two vintages I really stocked up on, 48 bottles of each for the cellar, we opened one of each with the interns during harvest and while 10 probably has the edge I’m very happy to have both.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
Andrew_Will:

Unrelated, but can we talk about how unmanageable the Winex website is? I would order from them more often if I could navigate the site…

Agreed. There are lots of issues with the website, and I’ve certainly had a few times when I couldn’t order something and just gave up. Also, their documentation of previous orders isn’t very user friendly. Your order info is well documented, but when you want to know what is in stock, you have to email them and get a ten page pdf of random orders. The shipments come with the same info printed out but handwritten notes excluding unavailable wines.

I just had an order missing a few bottles, but it was impossible to tell if they made a mistake on the packing slip or not since it’s just a printout of the order with handwritten notes on it. They’re investigating to see what went wrong, but I’m not sure how they would know if they messed up. I’ve had mostly great experiences and love the offerings, but I also learned from quitting Envoyer that the lowest price isn’t always worth it if I have to spend a lot of time helping coordinate shipping and consolidation.

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: Hello William, can you expound on the '10 as I am sitting on six bottles. Can it be enjoyed now? What would you recommend decant time? MTIA

posts.name: William Kelley
